name: Quadtree / Octree
def: A tree that recursively subdivides space into four (2-D quadtree) or eight (3-D octree) equal quadrants/octants.
problem: Index points or regions in 2-D/3-D for range, nearest-neighbour and collision queries, adapting resolution to data density.
mechanism: Each node covers an axis-aligned box; when it exceeds a capacity it splits into 4 (quad) or 8 (oct) equal children, so dense regions subdivide deeply while sparse regions stay shallow.
benefits: Natural for level-of-detail and view culling
limits: Wastes nodes on highly non-uniform data
Axis-aligned, fixed splits (less adaptive than a k-d tree)
Depth blows up for tightly clustered points
failures: Degenerate deep trees from coincident points
whenNot: High-dimensional data (use a k-d tree / ANN)
Indexing extents on disk (use an R-tree)
myths: "An octree is just a 3-D quadtree" — same idea, but fan-out and memory scale very differently
